Sergio Romo against Cabrera. Fastball right down the middle. Audacious.

Matt Cain's perfect game. The most perfect thing that's ever happened before noon.

The Thrill at first base. There's no better nickname in Giants history.
Will Clark's swing was slow-motion beautiful even in real time.
Robby Thompson turned double plays like he had something to prove.
Kevin Mitchell's one-handed homer was a myth before it was a memory.
Matt Williams hit it so hard sometimes the ball looked confused.
Rod Beck with that mustache and that slider. A genuine folk hero.
J.T. Snow's tag at the plate in 2002 is the most dramatic safe call I've ever seen.
Russ Ortiz tossed that ball into the stands in 2002 too soon. We don't talk about it.
Barry Bonds walked to the plate and the temperature in every stadium dropped.
Intentional walks: invented to stop Barry Bonds, unsuccessfully.
72 home runs. Let that number sit with you over your morning coffee.
Bonds made a 0-3 count feel like a personal challenge to the universe.
Watching Bonds hit was like watching someone cheat at physics.
Jeff Kent hit cleanup behind Bonds and was quietly devastating. Respect.
The Bonds-Kent dynamic was complicated. The baseball was beautiful.
Rich Aurilia's 2001 season was the most underrated thing in SF baseball history.
Livan Hernandez in the '97 playoffs. Untouchable and very slightly mysterious.
Bill Mueller won a batting title so quietly no one noticed until it was over.
Omar Vizquel at shortstop was ballet with dirt on it.

Andres Torres running down fly balls was the city's heartbeat.
Cody Ross in 2010 arrived like a rumor and stayed like a legend.
What even was Cody Ross? The best kind of surprise.
Aubrey Huff wore that rally thong all the way to a championship. Respect.
Pat Burrell's walk-off in 2010 was the most Pat Burrell thing imaginable.
Tim Lincecum in his prime was a man detached from the laws of mechanics.
Watching Lincecum's changeup was like watching a magic trick dissolve.
The Freak earned that name in the first inning of the first game he ever pitched.
Back-to-back Cy Youngs for Lincecum. The city floated for two years straight.
Ryan Vogelsong's comeback story is the kind of thing that makes baseball worth watching.
Matt Cain was so consistently excellent that we almost forgot to appreciate it.
Barry Zito in 2012 reminded us that unpredictability is sometimes the plan.
Jake Peavy mid-trade to a championship run. Baseball is stranger than fiction.
Buster Posey won a batting title from behind the plate. Multitasker of the decade.
Posey had the quiet authority of someone who always knew where the pitch was going.
When Buster called for the outside slider, you trusted him before the ball left the hand.
Brandon Belt waiting on a curveball was a form of meditation.
Brandon Crawford making a play in the hole looked like it violated local ordinances.
Joe Panik's pivot on the double play in 2014 is the smoothest thing I've ever seen on grass.
Angel Pagan's inside-the-park walk-off in 2012 was the most chaotic wonderful thing.
Hunter Pence running the bases is a man reinventing what running means.
Everything Hunter Pence did was slightly wrong and completely right.
Gregor Blanco's diving catch in 2012 kept Matt Cain's perfecto alive. Unsung hero.
Michael Morse homering in the rain was proof the baseball gods have taste.
Travis Ishikawa's walk-off in the 2014 NLCS was the best surprise since Cody Ross.

Bumgarner's five innings of relief in Game 7 is the most relentless thing in baseball history.
MadBum in 2014 was not a pitcher, he was a weather event.
The Kansas City catcher — Bumgarner still close enough to tag him. Barely. Forever.
Every sunrise since 2014 tastes a little like that final out.

Bochy managed with the energy of a man who had already seen it end well.

Bochy pulled that bullpen cord like a man conducting an orchestra only he could hear.

The Bay doesn't care about records. The Bay cares about heart.
Bonds at the plate, fog on the hills, everything possible.
From here, every baseball morning starts at the edge of the continent.
The Pacific ends here and the baseball begins. Seems right.
This city built its identity on gold rushes and Giants baseball. Both worth chasing.
There's a version of this morning where the Giants win. Let's be in that version.
Go Giants. Now drink your coffee.
